perturbation.original_text:
5.5 Insurance During the term of this Agreement, you shall maintain in force under policies of insurance issued by licensed insurers approved by us insurance coverage as we from time to time require. You must maintain insurance related to your operation of the Franchised Business. Such insurance coverage will include:
5.5.1 As it relates to the operation of your Franchised Business: broad form comprehensive general liability coverage against claims for employment practices coverage, bodily and personal injury, death and property damage caused by or occurring in conjunction with the conduct of business by you pursuant to this Agreement and broad form contractual liability coverage, including errors and omissions coverage, under one or more policies of insurance containing minimum liability coverage prescribed by us from time to time, but in no event in an amount less than Two Million Dollars ($2,000,000) aggregate. Such insurance shall not have a deductible or self-insured retention in excess of Five Thousand Dollars ($5,000);
perturbation.changed_text:
5.5 Insurance During the term of this Agreement, you shall maintain in force under policies of insurance issued by licensed insurers approved by us insurance coverage as we from time to time require. You must maintain insurance related to your operation of the Franchised Business.
5.5.1 As it relates to the operation of your Franchised Business: general liability coverage against claims for bodily and personal injury, death and property damage caused by or occurring in conjunction with the conduct of business by you pursuant to this Agreement under one or more policies of insurance.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'broad form comprehensive general liability coverage', 'employment practices coverage' and 'errors and omissions coverage' alongside the minimum liability coverage amount of $2,000,000, significantly weakens the insurance requirement, creating potential non-compliance with state laws that mandate specific minimum coverage amounts and types of coverage for commercial leases.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts NY General Business Law § 683.1(j), which requires franchisors to disclose all material facts relevant to the franchise, including insurance requirements. By removing specific coverage types and minimum amounts, the franchisor is not providing a clear and complete disclosure of the franchisee's insurance obligations, potentially misleading the franchisee about the extent of coverage needed and violating the disclosure requirements of the law.

2025-03-07 2025-03-28 2025-04-04 2025-05-16 2025-05-23 2025-05-30 2025-06-13 Share Facebook Email SECTION 683 Disclosure requirements General Business (GBS) CHAPTER 20, ARTICLE 33 § 683. Disclosure requirements. 1. It shall be unlawful and prohibited for any person to offer to sell or sell in this state any franchise unless and until there shall have been registered with the department of law, prior to such offer or sale, a written statement to be known as an "offering prospectus" concerning the contemplated offer or sale, which shall contain the information and representations set forth in and required by this section. Any uniform disclosure document approved for use by any agency of the federal government or sister state may be utilized and sought to be registered, provided that said uniform disclosure documents comply with the provisions of this article. 2. The offering prospectus sought to be registered with the department of law shall be filed with the department, accompanied by an application for registration on forms prescribed by the department, and shall contain the following: (a) The name of the franchisor, the name under which the franchisor is doing or intends to do business,
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes essential insurance coverage requirements (broad form comprehensive general liability coverage, employment practices coverage, and errors and omissions coverage) and a minimum liability coverage amount of $2,000,000, which the original contract mandated. The Law Explanation states this contradicts NY General Business Law § 683.1(j), which requires franchisors to disclose all material facts relevant to the franchise, including insurance requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY General Business Law, Article 33, § 683, supports this by outlining disclosure requirements for franchise offerings. By removing the specific coverage types and minimum amounts, the modified text potentially misleads the franchisee about their insurance obligations, creating a legal contradiction because it undermines the full disclosure mandated by law.

perturbation.original_text: 19.7 Trial by Jury WE AND YOU EACH HEREBY WAIVE OUR RESPECTIVE RIGHT TO TRIAL BY JURY OF ANY CAUSE OF ACTION, CLAIM, COUNTERCLAIM OR CROSS-COMPLAINT IN ANY ACTION, PROCEEDING AND/OR HEARING BROUGHT BY EITHER US OR YOU ON ANY MATTER WHATSOEVER ARISING OUT OF, OR IN ANY WAY CONNECTED WITH, THIS AGREEMENT, THE RELATIONSHIP OF THE PARTIES, THE USE OF THE PROPRIETARY MARKS OR SYSTEM BY YOU, OR ANY CLAIM OF INJURY OR DAMAGE, OR THE ENFORCEMENT OF ANY REMEDY UNDER ANY LAW, STATUTE, REGULATION, EMERGENCY OR OTHERWISE, NOW OR HEREAFTER IN EFFECT, TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED UNDER LAW.
perturbation.changed_text: 19.7 Trial by Jury WE AND YOU EACH HEREBY WAIVE OUR RESPECTIVE RIGHT TO TRIAL BY JURY OF ANY CAUSE OF ACTION, CLAIM, COUNTERCLAIM OR CROSS-COMPLAINT IN ANY ACTION, PROCEEDING AND/OR HEARING BROUGHT BY EITHER US OR YOU ON ANY MATTER WHATSOEVER ARISING OUT OF, OR IN ANY WAY CONNECTED WITH, THIS AGREEMENT, THE RELATIONSHIP OF THE PARTIES, THE USE OF THE PROPRIETARY MARKS OR SYSTEM BY YOU, OR ANY CLAIM OF INJURY OR DAMAGE, OR THE ENFORCEMENT OF ANY REMEDY UNDER ANY LAW, STATUTE, REGULATION, EMERGENCY OR OTHERWISE, NOW OR HEREAFTER IN EFFECT.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED UNDER LAW' from the jury waiver clause could be interpreted as an attempt to enforce a jury waiver even in situations where such a waiver is legally prohibited or restricted. This could violate state laws that require jury waivers to be knowing and voluntary, and only enforceable to the extent permitted by law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3513 states that anyone may waive the advantage of a law intended solely for his benefit. By omitting “to the fullest extent permitted under law”, the contract language now appears to require a jury trial waiver regardless of whether such a waiver is permissible under applicable law. In California, waivers must be knowing and voluntary. Removing that sentence could imply that the jury trial waiver applies regardless of what protections the law extends to jury trials.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 4. MAXIMS OF JURISPRUDENCE [3509 - 3548] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) 3513. Any one may waive the advantage of a law intended solely for their benefit. But a law established for a public reason cannot be contravened by a private agreement. (Amended by Stats. 2024, Ch. 812, Sec. 2. (AB 1899) Effective January 1, 2025.)
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED UNDER LAW' creates a legal contradiction. California Civil Code § 3513, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that anyone may waive the advantage of a law intended solely for his benefit. By removing the phrase that limits the waiver to the extent permitted by law, the contract now implies a complete waiver, regardless of legal limitations. This could contradict the requirement that waivers be knowing and voluntary, as the removal implies the waiver is absolute, and might apply even where legally impermissible. Therefore, the modified text weakens the protection afforded by law, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.7 Payment of Taxes You shall pay all personal property, sales, excise, use, and other taxes, regardless of type or nature, which may be imposed, levied, assessed or charged, on, against, or in connection with the Franchised Business and any products, services or equipment sold or furnished hereunder, whether those taxes are imposed by any federal, state, municipality, county or parish, or other governmental unity or agency, which may have jurisdiction over such products, services and equipment. It shall be your sole responsibility to insure that any Unit Franchisee operating in the Master Territory shall also comply with this Section 5.7 as it may apply to the operation of the Unit Franchisee's business.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.7 Payment of Taxes You shall pay all personal property, sales, excise, use, and other taxes, regardless of type or nature, which may be imposed, levied, assessed or charged, on, against, or in connection with the Franchised Business and any products, services or equipment sold or furnished hereunder. It shall be your sole responsibility to insure that any Unit Franchisee operating in the Master Territory shall also comply with this Section 5.7 as it may apply to the operation of the Unit Franchisee's business.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause specifying 'whether those taxes are imposed by any federal, state, municipality, county or parish, or other governmental unity or agency, which may have jurisdiction over such products, services and equipment' from the tax obligations clause may create ambiguity and shift the burden of determining applicable tax jurisdictions to the franchisee. Franchise tax laws require clear identification of responsibilities for remitting taxes and this shift could contravene franchise disclosure laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission contradicts 16 CFR § 436.5(a)(1)(x), which requires the franchise agreement to clearly outline the franchisee's tax obligations. By removing the clause that clarifies the scope of tax obligations across various jurisdictions, the agreement becomes less transparent about the franchisee's responsibilities for taxes imposed by federal, state, and local authorities. This lack of clarity violates the disclosure requirements of the Franchise Rule, potentially misleading the franchisee.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a critical clause specifying the jurisdictional scope of tax obligations, creating a potential conflict with 16 CFR § 436.5(a)(1)(x), as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The original text clearly states that the franchisee is responsible for taxes imposed by 'any federal, state, municipality, county or parish, or other governmental unity or agency,' providing clarity on the scope of tax obligations. By omitting this clause, the modified text becomes less transparent about the franchisee's responsibilities, potentially violating the disclosure requirements of the Franchise Rule. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this omission could shift the burden of determining applicable tax jurisdictions to the franchisee, which aligns with the concern that the franchisee might be misled due to the lack of clarity. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies 16 CFR 436.5 as related to 'Disclosure items,' thus supporting the need for clear disclosure of tax obligations. The omission directly contradicts the need for clear and comprehensive disclosure, increasing the likelihood of disputes regarding tax liabilities and compliance.
